questions.prompt: Why does `sed 's/foo/bar/' file.txt` only replace the first foo per line?

choices.text: That's sed's default behavior — `s/pattern/replacement/` replaces the first match on each line; the `g` (global) flag enables all matches per line. Use `s/foo/bar/g` for what most newcomers expect: replace every occurrence on every line. Other flags worth knowing: `i` for case-insensitive (GNU sed only), `2` to replace only the second occurrence on each line, `N` to replace only the Nth. The default-first behavior is occasionally useful — say you want only the first occurrence — but most real uses want /g. After this trips you up once, it's wired in forever. Test substitutions without `-i` first to verify the result before writing back to disk.

questions.explanation: Default replaces only first per line. Add `/g` flag for global replacement.

questions.prompt: On macOS, `sed -i 's/foo/bar/' file.txt` errors with 'extra characters at end of i command'. Fix?

choices.text: macOS uses BSD sed, which requires a backup-extension argument with `-i`. Two fixes: `sed -i '' 's/foo/bar/' file.txt` (empty string = no backup), or `sed -i.bak 's/foo/bar/' file.txt` (creates file.txt.bak). Linux GNU sed accepts `-i` alone — hence the confusion when scripts move between platforms. For cross-platform portability, always provide an extension (use `.bak` and delete the backup afterward if needed), or `gsed` on macOS (install via brew install gnu-sed) which behaves like Linux. Many CI/scripts in the wild fail on macOS for exactly this reason; making your sed commands portable is a small price for not debugging it later.

questions.explanation: BSD sed requires backup arg with -i. Use `sed -i '' ...` or `sed -i.bak ...`.
